The condition on g ‚â°1 in Assumption 3.1, part (2) is trivially satisfied. Finally, to verify (3.6), we prove the sufficient condition (3.7) holds. Even more simply, it suffices to show f(x) ‚â•Œ∫ min(‚à•x‚à•2/2, ‚àöœµ‚à•x‚à•) for all x ‚ààRd. Let c = maxu‚ààRd ‚à•‚àá3f(x)‚à•op, which we know is bounded, by the above calculation. Then for all ‚à•x‚à•‚â§‚àöœµ, a Taylor expansion gives f(x) ‚â•(1 ‚àíc‚àöœµ/3)‚à•x‚à•2/2 ‚â•1 2(‚à•x‚à•2/2), if œµ is small enough. For all ‚à•x‚à•‚â•‚àöœµ, convexity of f implies that f(x) ‚â•‚à•x‚à• ‚à•y‚à•f(y), where ‚à•y‚à•= ‚àöœµ and y lies on the line segment between 0 and x. But now we use that f(y) ‚â•‚à•y‚à•2/4 since ‚à•y‚à•= ‚àöœµ, so f(x) ‚â•‚à•x‚à•‚à•y‚à•/4 = ‚à•x‚à•‚àöœµ/4. Therefore, f(x) ‚â•Œ∫ min(‚à•x‚à•2/2, ‚àöœµ‚à•x‚à•) is satisfied on Rd, with Œ∫ = 1/4. Theorem 3.2 therefore applies to f from (8.7). As in Section 8.2, we can satisfy the lower bound in (3.8) by taking R = c for some constant c depending on CX, cx, œà, and L. we conclude that log Z Rd e‚àín‚Ñì(x)dx = ‚àín‚Ñì(x‚àó) ‚àí1 2 log det H ‚àíd 2 log n 2œÄ + b1 n + O d3 n2  , (8.9) whenever d3/n2 is sufficiently small. Here, the constant suppressed by the big-O depends on CX, cx, œà. The term b1 can be computed using the general formula (6.14), and similar calculations as in [22, 21]. Note that the righthand side of the first line in (8.9) is the Bayesian information criterion (BIC) [29]. Thus b1/n can be considered a higher-order correction to BIC, with the higher-order remainder explicitly controlled. References [1] Tomohiro Ando. Bayesian model selection and statistical modeling. A Structure-preserving tensor operations Recall from Section 2 the notation of a tensor Ak‚Üíj: a multilinear mapping from k vectors in Rd to either a scalar if j = 0, a vector in Rd if j = 1, and a d √ó d matrix if j = 2. Recall also the concept of a base tensor Gk‚Üíj and a composite tensor F œµ k‚Üíj. In the next lemma, we list some operations which preserve the structure of a composite tensor. Recall that all the composite tensors F œµ k‚Üíj and all the base tensors Gk‚Üíj they are composed of are symmetric. Lemma A.1. We have the following identities: (1) (œµ-scaling) œµpF œµ k‚Üíj = F œµ k‚Üíj if p ‚â•0. (2) (composition) F œµ n‚Üíj  F œµ k1‚Üí1[x‚äók1], . . . , F œµ kn‚Üí1[x‚äókn]  = F œµ K‚Üíj[x‚äóK], where K = k1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + kn. (3) (matrix multiplication) F œµ k1‚Üí2[x‚äók1]F œµ k2‚Üí2[x‚äók2] ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ F œµ k‚Ñì‚Üí2[x‚äók‚Ñì] = F œµ K‚Üí2[x‚äóK], where K = k1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + k‚Ñìand AB refers to matrix multiplication of A and B. (4) (trace) 1 dtr(F œµ k‚Üí2[x‚äók]) = F œµ k‚Üí0[x‚äók]. In each case, the equality should be read as follows: given the composite tensors appearing on each lefthand side, there exists a composite tensors of the form given on the righthand side to make the equality true. Remark A.2. We will have use for the third identity with k1 = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ = k‚Ñì= 0. The identity then gives that the product of matrices of the form P ‚Ñìœµ‚ÑìG(‚Ñì) 0‚Üí2 is also such a matrix. Proof. The first identity is trivial: clearly the structure is preserved, and boundedness of operator norms is unaffected. To prove the second identity, multilinearity gives that the lefthand side is a sum of nonnegative powers of œµ times terms of the form Gn‚Üíj h Gk1‚Üí1[x‚äók1], . . . , Gkn‚Üí1[x‚äókn] i . (A.1) Define TK‚Üíj[x1, . . . , xK] by TK‚Üíj[x1, . . . , xK] = 1 K! X œÉ Gn‚Üíj h Gk1‚Üí1[xœÉ(1), . . . , xœÉ(k1)], Gk2‚Üí1[xœÉ(k1+1), . . . , xœÉ(k1+k2)], . . . , Gkn‚Üí1[xœÉ(K‚àíkn+1), . . . , xœÉ(K)] i , (A.2) where the sum is over all permutations œÉ of {1, . . . , K}. Then TK‚Üíj satisfies TK‚Üíj[x‚äóK] = Gn‚Üíj h Gk1‚Üí1[x‚äók1], . . . , Gkn‚Üí1[x‚äókn] i . (A.3) It remains to show TK‚Üíj is a base tensor. It is symmetric by construction, and only depends on d, ‚àámf(0), ‚àám log g(0) since this is true for each of Gn‚Üíj, Gk1‚Üí1, . . . , Gkn‚Üí1. Furthermore, we have ‚à•TK‚Üíj‚à•op ‚â§‚à•Gn‚Üíj‚à•op‚à•Gk1‚Üí1‚à•op . . . ‚à•Gkn‚Üí1‚à•op ‚â§c. 33

To prove the third identity, multilinearity gives that the lefthand side is a sum of nonnegative powers of œµ times terms of the form Gk1‚Üí2[x‚äók1]Gk2‚Üí2[x‚äók2] ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ Gk‚Ñì‚Üí2[x‚äók‚Ñì] for base tensors Gk1‚Üí2, . . . , Gk‚Ñì‚Üí2. As in (A.2), define a symmetric tensor TK‚Üí2 such that TK‚Üí2[x‚äóK] = Gk1‚Üí2[x‚äók1]Gk2‚Üí2[x‚äók2] ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ Gk‚Ñì‚Üí2[x‚äók‚Ñì]. (Recall that symmetry of TK‚Üí2 is with respect to the input arguments. The output matrix need not be symmetric.) It remains to show TK‚Üí2 is a base tensor. It is symmetric by construction, and only depends on d, ‚àámf(0), ‚àám log g(0) since this is true for each Gk1‚Üí2, . . . , Gk‚Ñì‚Üí2. Furthermore, we have ‚à•TK‚Üí2‚à•op ‚â§‚à•Gk1‚Üí2‚à•op . . . ‚à•Gk‚Ñì‚Üí2‚à•op < c. (A.4) The fourth identity is straightforward. Corollary A.3. Let K be a finite subset of N ‚à™{0}. It holds F œµ n‚Üíj " X k‚ààK F œµ k‚Üí1[x‚äók] !‚äón# = X q‚ààQ F œµ q‚Üíj[x‚äóq], (A.5) and  X k‚ààK F œµ k‚Üí2[x‚äók] n = X q‚ààQ F œµ q‚Üí2[x‚äóq], (A.6) for some finite subset Q ‚äÇN ‚à™{0}, where min{q : q ‚ààQ} = n ¬∑ min{k : k ‚ààK}. Proof. (A.5) follows from multilinearity of the ‚äón operation and the second identity of Lemma A.1. Similarly, (A.6) follows from multilinearity of the matrix power operation, and the third identity of Lemma A.1. Lemma A.4. Let A(t) = P k‚â•1 F œµ k‚Üí2[t‚äók], m ‚â•0, and r ‚â§1 be small enough. Then there are composite tensors F œµ k such that for any N ‚â•2, we have log det(Id + œµmA(t)) = dœµm  N‚àí1 X k=1 F œµ k‚Üí0[t‚äók] + O �‚à•t‚à•N ! , ‚àÄ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. (A.7) Proof. As is well known, log det(Id + œµmA(t)) = tr log(Id + œµmA(t)). Due to the form of A(t) and the assumption r ‚â§1, we have ‚à•œµmA(t)‚à•op ‚â§‚à•A(t)‚à•op ‚â≤‚à•t‚à•for all ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. We assume r is sufficiently small that ‚à•œµmA(t)‚à•op ‚â§1/2 for all ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. We then have log(Id + œµmA(t)) ‚àíœµm  N‚àí1 X k=1 (‚àí1)k+1 k œµm(k‚àí1)A(t)k  op ‚â§ ‚àû X k=N ‚à•œµmA(t)‚à•k k ‚â≤‚à•œµmA(t)‚à•N ‚â≤œµmN‚à•t‚à•N, ‚àÄ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. (A.8) By the first identity in Lemma A.1, and (A.6) in Corollary A.3, the sum in curly braces can be expressed as P ‚Ñì‚â•1 F œµ ‚Ñì‚Üí2[t‚äó‚Ñì]. Furthermore, we have ‚à•P ‚Ñì‚â•N F œµ ‚Ñì‚Üí2[t‚äó‚Ñì]‚à•‚â≤‚à•t‚à•N for all ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. Therefore, log(Id + œµmA(t)) ‚àíœµm N‚àí1 X ‚Ñì=1 F œµ ‚Ñì‚Üí2[t‚äó‚Ñì]
op ‚â≤œµm‚à•t‚à•N ‚àÄ‚à•t‚à•‚â§r. (A.9) Using that |trA ‚àítrB| ‚â§d‚à•A ‚àíB‚à•, and using the fourth identity in Lemma A.1 concludes the proof. 34

Lemma A.5. Let F œµ k1‚Üí1, F œµ k2‚Üí1, . . . , F œµ k‚Ñì‚Üí1 be composite tensors, with ki ‚â•2 for all i, and let ti(s) = s + œµmF œµ ki‚Üí1[s‚äóki], i = 1, . . . , ‚Ñì, where m ‚â•0. Then there exist F œµ p‚Üí1, p ‚â•2, such that (t1 ‚ó¶t2 ‚ó¶¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚ó¶t‚Ñì)(s) = s + œµm X p‚â•2 F œµ p‚Üí1[s‚äóp]. (A.10) Proof. We use induction. The result trivially holds for ‚Ñì= 1. Suppose (A.10) holds for some ‚Ñì‚àí1 ‚â•1. Then (t1 ‚ó¶t2 ‚ó¶¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚ó¶t‚Ñì‚àí1)(t‚Ñì(s)) = t‚Ñì(s) + œµm X p‚â•2 F œµ p‚Üí1[t‚Ñì(s)‚äóp] = s + œµm  F œµ k‚Ñì‚Üí1[s‚äók‚Ñì] + X p‚â•2 F œµ p‚Üí1  s + œµmF œµ k‚Ñì‚Üí1[s‚äók‚Ñì] ‚äóp  = s + œµm X q‚â•2 F œµ q‚Üí1[s‚äóq]. (A.11) The last line (including that q ‚â•2) is by the assumption k‚Ñì‚â•2 and by (A.5) of Corollary A.3. B Change of variable proofs Proof of Lemma 5.4. Recall that œÜ(0) = 0. The assumption ‚à•œÜ‚Ä≤(t)‚à•op ‚â§1/2, ‚àÄ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r, (B.1) implies that ‚à•œÜ(t)‚à•‚â§‚à•t‚à•/2, ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r. This gives ‚à•(id + œÜ)(t)‚à•‚â§3 2‚à•t‚à•‚â§C1r, ‚àÄ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2 3C1r. (B.2) We conclude {‚à•t‚à•‚â§2 3C1r} ‚äÇX‚àí1(U). Since U ‚äÇ{‚à•x‚à•‚â§C2r}, we will finish the proof of (1) and prove (2) by showing that for any ‚à•x‚à•‚â§C2r there exists a unique ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r such that X(t) = x. Fix any such x and define œÜx(t) = x ‚àíœÜ(t). We have œÜx({‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r}) ‚äÇ{‚à•u‚à•‚â§2C2r}, (B.3) because ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r and (B.1) imply ‚à•œÜx(t)‚à•‚â§‚à•x‚à•+ ‚à•œÜ(t) ‚àíœÜ(0)‚à•‚â§C2r + 1 2‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r. (B.4) Furthermore, again using (B.1), it is straightforward to show that œÜx is a strict contraction on {‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r}. Therefore, the contraction mapping theorem shows there is a unique ‚à•t‚à•‚â§2C2r satisfying œÜx(t) = t. But then x = (id + œÜ)(t), proving the claims. For the proof of Lemma 5.3, we state and prove a main auxiliary lemma. Recall that F œµ k is shorthand for F œµ k‚Üí0. Lemma B.1. Let 3 ‚â§M ‚â§2L + 1 and f be a function on Rd given by f(x) = 1 2‚à•x‚à•2 + X k‚â•M F œµ k[x‚äók] (B.5) Then there exists a composite tensor F œµ (M‚àí1)‚Üí1 such that f  x ‚àíF œµ (M‚àí1)‚Üí1[x‚äóM‚àí1]  = 1 2‚à•x‚à•2 + X k‚â•M+1 F œµ k[x‚äók]. (B.6) 35

Proof. Let F œµ M be the specific composite tensor appearing in (B.5). For each x, let A(M‚àí1)‚Üí1[x‚äóM‚àí1] be the vector such that A(M‚àí1)‚Üí1[x‚äóM‚àí1]‚ä§u = F œµ M[x‚äóM‚àí1, u] for all u. The order of the M argu- ments input to F œµ M[¬∑] is irrelevant, since F œµ M is symmetric by definition. It is straightforward to see that A(M‚àí1)‚Üí1 is composite, so from now on we call it F œµ (M‚àí1)‚Üí1. Let p(x) = ‚àíF œµ (M‚àí1)‚Üí1[x‚äóM‚àí1]. Then x‚ä§p(x) = ‚àíF œµ M[x‚äóM], by definition of F œµ (M‚àí1)‚Üí1. Thus 1 2‚à•x + p(x)‚à•2 = 1 2‚à•x‚à•2 ‚àíF œµ M[x‚äóM] + (1 2Id)[p(x)‚äó2]. Here, we are identifying the matrix Id with the bilinear form Id[u ‚äóu] = Pd i,j=1(Id)ijuiuj = ‚à•u‚à•2. We then have f(x + p(x)) =1 2‚à•x‚à•2 +  F œµ M[(x + p(x))‚äóM ‚àíx‚äóM] + (1 2Id)[p(x)‚äó2] + X k‚â•M+1 F œµ k[(x + p(x))‚äók]  . (B.7) We expand the outer products in the terms inside the curly braces. By Corollary A.3, the result of doing these outer product expansions is a sum of the form P q F œµ q [x‚äóq]. It suffices to show q ‚â•M +1 for all q in the sum. For (1 2Id)[p(x)‚äó2], we have q = 2M ‚àí2 ‚â•M +1, since M ‚â•3. For F œµ k[(x+p(x))‚äók], k ‚â•M +1, all resulting F œµ q [x‚äóq] have q ‚â•M + 1. Finally, expanding the outer product, F œµ M[(x + p(x))‚äóM ‚àí x‚äóM] is a sum of terms of the form F œµ M[x‚äóM‚àím ‚äóp(x)‚äóm] = F œµ q [x‚äóq] (by (A.5) with j = 0) for q = (M ‚àím) + (M ‚àí1)m = M + (M ‚àí2)m, with m = 1, . . . , M. Since m ‚â•1 we have q ‚â•2M ‚àí2 ‚â•M + 1. Proof of Lemma 5.3. Note that f2L+1 satisfies the conditions of Lemma B.1 with M = 3. In fact, the tensors in the expansion of f2L+1 are base tensors, which are a special case of composite tensors.
